role_summary: Queens College anthropology student and Freedom Summer volunteer; arrived in Mississippi June 20, 1964 for his CORE Freedom Summer orientation, was killed the following day, June 21, 1964, alongside James Chaney and Michael Schwerner near Philadelphia, Mississippi by Ku Klux Klan members organized by Neshoba County Deputy Sheriff Cecil Price.
ai_reading: Goodman is named across the corpus chiefly as one-third of an inseparable triad: the corrected transcripts almost never say his name alone, but as part of the set Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney, and several interviewees who reach for the trio recover only two names cleanly and supply the third as the volunteer they cannot quite place. Cleveland Sellers (entry 22) and the Ladner sisters (entry 75) both narrate seeing the three off from the Oxford, Ohio orientation, and both name Schwerner and Chaney as the experienced organizers while marking Goodman specifically as the volunteer who had not yet been to Mississippi. Euvester Simpson (entry 41) states the asymmetry plainly: she had met Chaney and Schwerner but not Andrew, because he was a summer volunteer going down. That recurring naming pattern, two known organizers plus an unplaced third, is the embedding-legible trace of Goodman's single day in the state. The novel observation his page surfaces is the parental-loss-as-foundation-creation signature: Goodman's parents Carolyn and Robert Goodman founded the Andrew Goodman Foundation in 1966 to sustain student civic-engagement work, the same post-death institutional-perpetuation pattern visible in the Mamie Till-Mobley arc (Emmett Till external_figure page).
biographical_paragraph: Goodman was born November 23, 1943 in New York City to Robert and Carolyn Goodman, a politically active and prosperous Jewish family. He attended Walden School and enrolled at Queens College in fall 1962, where he studied anthropology and became active in the campus civil rights organizing. He volunteered with the Congress of Racial Equality for the 1964 Mississippi Summer Project (Freedom Summer) and traveled to Oxford, Ohio, in mid-June for the orientation training Bob Moses ran from Western College for Women. He left Ohio on June 20, 1964 and arrived in Meridian, Mississippi the same evening; on the morning of June 21 he, James Chaney, and Michael Schwerner drove to the Longdale community of Neshoba County to investigate the Klan firebombing of the Mt. Zion Methodist Church. The three were arrested by Neshoba County Deputy Sheriff Cecil Price for an alleged speeding violation, held in the Philadelphia jail until nightfall, then released into a Klan ambush that killed all three. Goodman was twenty. Their bodies were recovered from an earthen dam on August 4, 1964; his parents founded the Andrew Goodman Foundation in 1966 to support college-student civic engagement, the institutional legacy that continues today.

interview_snippets.speaker: Euvester Simpson
interview_snippets.lead_in: Mississippi SNCC worker Euvester Simpson recalls that she had met two of the three men before the murders but never the third, Andrew Goodman, who reached Mississippi only as the summer project began:
interview_snippets.quote: And before I left, though, we got word that James Chaney and Michael "Mickey" Schwerner and The three Freedom Summer victims, I had not met Andrew because he was one of the summer volunteers going down. And he left early from the orientation to go back to Meridian with Michael "Mickey" Schwerner and with James.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dr. Cleveland Sellers
interview_snippets.lead_in: SNCC organizer Cleveland Sellers, at the Oxford, Ohio orientation when word came that a church had been burned near Philadelphia, Mississippi, describes Goodman volunteering to drive back with Schwerner and Chaney to investigate:
interview_snippets.quote: And Chaney and they had to pick someone a volunteer to go along with them. And they wanted to pick a volunteer to go along with them. And so they got Goodman as a volunteer who volunteered to go. And they were going to go back and investigate the church burning. And so they left. And we actually saw him off.

interview_snippets.speaker: Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons
interview_snippets.lead_in: Freedom Summer project director Gwendolyn Zoharah Simmons, who had met James Chaney during her first week, recalls the orientation plenary where Bob Moses told the volunteers the missing men were probably dead:
interview_snippets.quote: I just, I couldn't believe it. I was like dead. I mean, you know, because I had met, uh, James Chaney, uh, that first week. And it was like, oh, these people really will kill us. I mean, it was like, even whites, they will kill whites, you know, white males.

interview_snippets.speaker: William Lamar Strickland
interview_snippets.lead_in: Northern Student Movement organizer William Lamar Strickland names the three to date the moment the federal posture toward protecting civil rights workers became explicit:
interview_snippets.quote: And then 64 after [Michael "Mickey"] Schwerner, [James] Chaney, [Andrew] Goodman, they kill J. Edgar Hoover says that they can't protect civil rights workers.

interview_snippets.speaker: Dorie Ann Ladner and Joyce Ann Ladner
interview_snippets.lead_in: In their joint interview, the Ladner sisters recall having seen the three men at the Oxford, Ohio orientation just before they returned to Mississippi to investigate the church burning, with Goodman marked as the volunteer new to the state:
interview_snippets.quote: And I had seen them at Miami University in Oxford, Ohio, as they were leaving to return to Mississippi because they had, we'd gotten this call that the church had burned and [Andrew Goodman, Mickey Schwerner] and James [Chaney] were, what, immediately returned.

interview_snippets.speaker: Ekwueme Michael Thelwell
interview_snippets.lead_in: MFDP Washington-office organizer Ekwueme Michael Thelwell describes how the murders of the three became the lever that opened Northern state Democratic conventions to the Mississippi Freedom Democratic Party challenge:
interview_snippets.quote: Actually, we got in the door because of Goodman, Schwerner, Chaney, because we took all those things to the National State Conventions,

sources.title: Seth Cagin and Philip Dray, We Are Not Afraid: The Story of Goodman, Schwerner, and Chaney and the Civil Rights Campaign for Mississippi
